title: Sibling Inheritance Wars: The Body's Ledger of What Was Owed
dek: When the estate is settled, the real accounting begins in the fascia. Your jaw remembers the slights from childhood, and the division of assets is never just about the money—it is about who was seen, who was loved, and who was left holding the tension in their psoas.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Reading of the Will is a Somatic Event

You are sitting in a room that smells of old paper and stale coffee, or perhaps you are on a Zoom call, staring at the pixelated faces of the people you shared a bathroom with for eighteen years. The lawyer is speaking. The words are legal, dry, and precise. But your body is not hearing legalities. Your body is hearing a verdict on your worth.

Notice your jaw right now. The masseter muscle, the thick band that connects your cheekbone to your lower jaw, is likely tight enough to crack a walnut. This is not a metaphor. This is biology. When the resources of the family are being divided, the mammalian brain perceives a threat to survival. The jaw clenches to prepare for a fight. The trapezius muscles, those broad triangles spanning from your neck to your shoulders, hike up toward your ears. You are bracing for impact.

The inheritance war is never about the antique clock or the summer cabin or the exact percentage of the mutual funds. It is about the ledger of love. It is about the invisible accounting that has been running in the background of your family dynamic since you were old enough to notice who got the bigger piece of cake. And now, the final tally is being read aloud.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his foundational work on trauma, reminds us that the body keeps the score. But in the case of sibling inheritance, the body is not just keeping the score; it is auditing the entire history of the family. Every slight, every preference, every time you were told you were "the strong one" while your sibling was "the sensitive one" (which meant they got the grace and you got the burden)—it is all stored in your fascia. The connective tissue remembers.

## The Psoas Knows Who Was the Favorite

Let us talk about the psoas. This deep core muscle connects your lumbar spine to your femur. It is the muscle of flight or fight, the one that curls you into a fetal position when you are attacked. When the lawyer announces that the sibling who barely visited during the final years is receiving the lion's share of the liquid assets, your psoas contracts. It pulls your pelvis forward, tightening your lower back. You might feel a sudden, sharp ache in your lumbar region.

This is your body recognizing an ancient injustice. You were the one who managed the hospice care. You were the one who changed the bedpans and listened to the death rattle. You were the one who held the space. And yet, the resources are flowing elsewhere. The anger that rises in you is not petty. It is not greedy. It is a profound, biological response to a violation of reciprocity.

Do not let anyone tell you that you should "just let it go" or that "it's only money." That is a spiritual bypass. That is a denial of your somatic reality. Your anger is a messenger, and it is telling you that your labor was unseen. It is telling you that the family system is operating on a distorted economy of affection.

When the heat rises in your chest, when your breath becomes shallow and trapped in your upper ribs, you might reach for a cup of [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20). The chamomile is not a cure for the injustice, but it is a gentle hand on the nervous system. It whispers to the vagus nerve that you are safe in this moment, even if the family structure is collapsing around you.

## The Myth of the Fair Share

There is a fantasy that death will bring clarity, that the reading of the will will finally balance the scales. But the scales were broken long ago. The parents who could not love you equally in life cannot love you equally in death. The document they left behind is just a continuation of their limitations.

Gabor Maté teaches us that addiction and dysfunction are rooted in a lack of attunement in childhood. When parents cannot attune to their children, they often substitute material goods or rigid rules for genuine connection. The inheritance becomes the ultimate substitute. It is the final, concrete manifestation of their inability to truly see you.

When your sibling fights you for the silver tea set, they are not fighting for the silver. They are fighting for the mother who never really held them. They are fighting for the father who was always at work. And when you fight back, you are doing the same. You are two starving children fighting over a crumb, while the ghosts of your parents watch from the shadows.

Notice the tension in your hands. Are your fingers curled into fists? The flexor muscles in your forearms are engaged, ready to grab, ready to hold on. This is the grasping reflex, an infant's desperate attempt to secure attachment. When the inheritance is threatened, we regress. We become the child who fears they will be left out in the cold.

## The Somatics of Betrayal

Betrayal has a specific physical signature. It feels like a sudden drop in the stomach, a hollowing out of the solar plexus. The diaphragm spasms, making it difficult to draw a full breath. The world narrows to a tunnel. This is the sympathetic nervous system kicking into overdrive.

When a sibling lies about an asset, or manipulates the executor, or suddenly produces a revised will, the betrayal cuts deeper than any stranger's deceit. This is the person who shares your DNA, who shares your history. The betrayal is a rupture in the foundational fabric of your reality.

In these moments, the anger can be terrifying. It can feel like a fire that will consume you. You might feel the heat radiating from your chest up into your neck and face. Your heart rate accelerates. This is the energy of mobilization. Your body is giving you the fuel you need to protect yourself.

Do not suppress this fire. Do not swallow it down into your belly, where it will fester and turn into resentment or illness. Let the fire burn, but give it a container.

## A Practice for the Fire

When the anger is too big for the room, when the emails from the lawyers are making your vision blur, try this.

Stand up. Plant your feet firmly on the ground, hip-width apart. Feel the solid earth beneath you. This is your foundation. The family may be shifting sand, but the earth is stable.

Bring your awareness to your legs. Feel the strength in your quadriceps and your calves. You are an adult. You are no longer the helpless child who had to accept whatever scraps of affection were offered.

Now, bring your attention to your jaw. Intentionally clench it as hard as you can for three seconds. One. Two. Three. Now, release it. Let the lower jaw drop. Wiggle it from side to side.

Take a deep breath into your belly, expanding the diaphragm. As you exhale, make a sound. A low, guttural sigh. Let the sound carry the vibration of the anger out of your body.

If you need support, consider rubbing a drop of [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) on your wrists. The scent of lavender has been shown to modulate the autonomic nervous system, helping to down-regulate the sympathetic response. It is a small anchor in the storm.

## The Boundary of the Skin

As the inheritance war drags on—and they always drag on—you must learn to define where you end and your sibling begins. The boundary of the skin is your first line of defense.

When we are entangled in family conflict, our energetic boundaries become porous. We absorb the anxiety, the greed, and the panic of our relatives. We feel their emotions as if they were our own. This is a survival mechanism from childhood, when we had to hyper-vigilantly monitor the moods of our caregivers to stay safe.

But you are safe now. You must reclaim your skin.

Notice the sensation of your clothing against your body. Notice the temperature of the air on your face. This is your container. What is inside this container belongs to you. What is outside belongs to them.

When your sibling sends a vitriolic text message, notice how your body wants to merge with their chaos. Your heart rate spikes to match theirs. Your breathing becomes shallow to match theirs. Stop. Return to your skin. Return to your breath.

You might find that [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be a supportive ally in maintaining this boundary. Skullcap is a nervine tonic, traditionally used to soothe a frayed nervous system and help establish a sense of internal quiet when the external world is loud.

## The Grief Beneath the Rage

Anger is the bodyguard of grief. When the inheritance war finally ends, when the papers are signed and the checks are cut, the anger will begin to subside. And beneath it, you will find the grief.

The grief is not for the money. The grief is for the family you never had. The grief is for the realization that your parents were flawed, limited human beings who could not give you what you needed. The grief is for the sibling relationship that has been irreparably broken by the process.

This grief is heavy. It settles in the lungs. In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the lungs are the organ associated with grief. You might find yourself sighing frequently, or feeling a weight on your chest. You might catch a cold that you cannot shake.

Allow the grief to move through you. Do not rush to "get over it." The body needs time to process the loss. The fascia needs time to unwind the tension it has been holding for months, or years.

Resmaa Menakem, in his work on somatic abolitionism, speaks of "clean pain" versus "dirty pain." Clean pain is the pain that mends and builds your capacity for growth. It is the pain of feeling the grief, setting the boundary, and walking away. Dirty pain is the pain of avoidance, of blame, of continuing the cycle of dysfunction.

Choose the clean pain. Feel the grief of the inheritance war, and let it change you. Let it teach you what you truly value. Let it show you that your worth is not determined by a legal document or a bank transfer.

## The Final Accounting

In the end, the only ledger that matters is the one within your own body. Have you honored your truth? Have you protected your nervous system? Have you allowed your anger to speak without letting it destroy you?

The money will be spent. The antique clock will eventually break. The summer cabin will be sold to strangers. But your body will remain with you until your final breath.

Tend to your body. Listen to its wisdom. When the jaw clenches, when the psoas tightens, when the breath catches—pay attention. These are the true inheritance. This is the legacy of survival, of resilience, of the profound intelligence of the human organism.

You are not the sum of what you were left in the will. You are the breath moving through your lungs. You are the blood pumping through your veins. You are the fierce, unapologetic life force that refuses to be diminished by the limitations of others.

## The Echoes in the Fascia

When we speak of the body's memory, we are not speaking in poetry. We are speaking of the fascial network, the continuous web of connective tissue that wraps every muscle, bone, nerve, artery, and vein in your body. This web is highly innervated. It is a sensory organ in its own right. And it is where the unresolved conflicts of the inheritance war take up residence.

Imagine the fascia as a three-dimensional sweater. When you pull on one thread, the tension is felt throughout the entire garment. When your sibling makes a passive-aggressive comment about the appraisal of the family home, the tension does not just stay in your ears. It travels down your neck, into your shoulders, and pulls at the fascia around your heart. 

This is why you might feel a sudden restriction in your chest, a difficulty in taking a full, expansive breath. The fascia is tightening, creating a physical armor against the emotional assault. This armoring is a brilliant survival strategy in the short term. It keeps you from collapsing under the weight of the betrayal. But in the long term, chronic fascial tension restricts blood flow, limits mobility, and keeps the nervous system locked in a state of high alert.

To begin unwinding this tension, you must meet it with curiosity rather than force. You cannot stretch the fascia into submission. You must invite it to melt. 

Find a quiet space where you will not be interrupted. Lie down on your back with your knees bent and your feet flat on the floor. Place one hand on your heart and one hand on your belly. Do not try to change your breathing; simply observe it. Notice where the breath gets stuck. Notice where the body feels dense or rigid.

As you lie there, bring to mind a moment from the inheritance process that felt particularly unjust. Notice what happens in your body. Does your stomach tighten? Does your throat constrict? Do your hands form fists? 

Acknowledge the sensation. Say to yourself, "Ah, there is the anger in my throat. There is the betrayal in my stomach." By naming the sensation, you bring the prefrontal cortex online, which helps to modulate the emotional intensity of the amygdala. 

Now, imagine sending a warm, golden light to that area of tension. Not to fix it, not to make it go away, but simply to hold it in a space of compassionate awareness. This is the essence of somatic healing. It is the willingness to be present with the discomfort without trying to escape it.

## The Legacy of Scarcity

The ferocity of an inheritance war is rarely about the actual monetary value of the assets. It is about the legacy of scarcity that has been passed down through the generations. 

If your parents grew up during the Depression, or if they experienced poverty, war, or displacement, their nervous systems were wired for survival. They learned that resources are scarce and that safety depends on hoarding what you have. This somatic imprint of scarcity is transmitted to the children, not just through words, but through the tone of voice, the tension in the household, the unspoken anxieties about money.

When the parents die, this inherited scarcity mindset is activated in the children. The fight over the estate is a fight for survival, driven by the ancestral fear that there will not be enough. 

Recognizing this generational trauma does not excuse your sibling's behavior, but it can provide a context that softens the edges of your anger. They are not just fighting you; they are fighting the ghosts of your ancestors. They are caught in a somatic loop of terror that they do not understand.

You, however, have the opportunity to break the cycle. You can choose to step out of the scarcity paradigm. You can recognize that your safety does not depend on the silver tea set or the mutual funds. Your safety depends on your connection to your own body, your capacity to regulate your nervous system, and your ability to source your worth from within.

This is the true inheritance. The ability to stand in the fire of family conflict and remain grounded in your own truth. The ability to feel the anger, the grief, and the betrayal, and to let them move through you without destroying you. 

When you do this, you are not just healing yourself. You are healing the ancestral line. You are changing the somatic legacy that you will pass down to the next generation. You are declaring that the buck stops here. The war is over. The body is finally at peace.

## FAQ

**Q: My sibling is the executor and is clearly hiding assets. My body is in a constant state of panic. What do I do?**
**A:** First, tend to the panic. Your body is reacting to a profound lack of safety and trust. Ground yourself physically before you take any legal action. Feel your feet on the floor. Breathe into your belly. Once your nervous system is somewhat regulated, hire your own lawyer. Do not try to fight this battle using only your own depleted resources. You need a professional boundary between you and your sibling's dysfunction.

**Q: I feel guilty for being so angry about the money. Shouldn't I be more spiritual about this?**
**A:** Guilt is often a learned response to our own vital energy. You were likely taught that anger is "bad" or "unspiritual." But anger is simply the biological response to a boundary violation. It is the energy of self-protection. Do not bypass your biology in the name of spirituality. Your anger is valid. It is telling you that something is unfair. Listen to it.

**Q: The inheritance was settled years ago, but my jaw still clenches when I think about my sister. How do I release this?**
**A:** The body does not know that the legal battle is over. The fascia is still holding the bracing pattern. You need to communicate safety to your body in the present moment. Somatic experiencing, massage, or simply the practice of intentionally clenching and releasing the jaw can help. Acknowledge the anger when it arises, remind your body that the threat has passed, and gently invite the muscles to soften.

**Q: Is it possible to repair the relationship with my sibling after an inheritance war?**
**A:** It is possible, but it requires both parties to be willing to do the deep, uncomfortable work of acknowledging the underlying wounds. If your sibling is still operating from a place of grasping and denial, you cannot force a repair. Focus on repairing the relationship with yourself. Focus on healing your own nervous system. That is the only repair you can control.
